Provisioned Throughput is owned by the account that purchases it, so centralizing inference in a shared services account and granting cross-account/endpoint access lets teams share one commitment and consolidates spend. Buying per-account commitments fragments and wastes capacity, SCPs cannot pool capacity, and there is no Organizations feature that automatically shares model units.

Source: Amazon Bedrock — Provisioned Throughput — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/prov-throughput.html

Question: A team wants to detect, in near real time, when a particular Bedrock model starts returning elevated client or server errors so they can fail over. Which is the most direct operational signal?
Answer Option 1: Parse the S3 invocation logs hourly with Athena and alert on error strings
Answer Option 2: Poll CloudTrail every minute for failed InvokeModel events
Answer Option 3: Create CloudWatch alarms on the InvocationClientErrors / InvocationServerErrors (and InvocationThrottles) metrics in the AWS/Bedrock namespace
Answer Option 4: Watch the Billing metric for a sudden drop in charges
Overall Explanation:
Bedrock emits client-error, server-error, and throttle metrics to CloudWatch, so alarms on them give near-real-time error detection for failover. Athena over S3 logs is hourly and slow, CloudTrail polling is delayed and not designed for live alarms, and billing changes are a lagging, indirect indicator.

Source: Amazon Bedrock — Model invocation logging — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-invocation-logging.html

Question: A data-privacy officer asks whether enabling Bedrock model invocation logging changes how Bedrock uses prompts and completions, and where the logged content resides. What is the accurate statement?
Answer Option 1: Enabling logging opts your data into base-model training
Answer Option 2: Logged content is stored in an AWS-owned account accessible to model providers
Answer Option 3: Logging automatically shares prompts with the foundation model provider for quality review
Answer Option 4: Invocation logging stores prompts/completions only in the customer's own S3 bucket and/or CloudWatch log group; it does not cause AWS to use that content to train base models
Overall Explanation:
Invocation logs are delivered to the customer's own S3 bucket and/or CloudWatch log group, and Bedrock does not use customer prompts or completions to train base models. Logging is an audit feature, not a consent to training, and the content is not exposed to AWS-owned accounts or model providers.

Source: Amazon Bedrock — Model invocation logging — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-invocation-logging.html

Question: Your application's per-account on-demand request and token-per-minute quotas for a model are consistently the binding constraint at peak, but traffic is steady all day. Comparing remedies, which is the most cost-effective long-term fix?
Answer Option 1: Move the steady high-volume portion to Provisioned Throughput, which provides guaranteed capacity independent of on-demand account quotas
Answer Option 2: Repeatedly request on-demand quota increases as traffic grows
Answer Option 3: Shard the workload across many IAM users to multiply the quotas
Answer Option 4: Add more retries with longer backoff to push more through the existing quota
Overall Explanation:
For steady, high, predictable volume, Provisioned Throughput supplies guaranteed capacity decoupled from on-demand account quotas and is the durable, cost-effective fix. Quota increases are not guaranteed and lag growth, on-demand quotas are account/model-scoped so splitting across IAM users does not multiply them, and more backoff only slows requests without adding capacity.

Source: Amazon Bedrock — Provisioned Throughput — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/prov-throughput.html

Question: An evaluation job must score models on a sensitive internal dataset that cannot leave a specific encrypted S3 location, and the results report must also be encrypted with the team's KMS key. When configuring a Bedrock model evaluation job, what should you ensure?
Answer Option 1: Embed the dataset inline in the job request to avoid using S3 at all
Answer Option 2: Reference the dataset in the approved S3 location, grant the evaluation service role read access and KMS decrypt, and specify the customer managed KMS key for the output results
Answer Option 3: Disable encryption on the output bucket because evaluation results are not sensitive
Answer Option 4: Copy the dataset to a public bucket so the evaluation service can read it
Overall Explanation:
The evaluation job's service role needs read access and KMS decrypt for the source data, and you can specify a customer managed KMS key to encrypt the output, keeping sensitive data and results protected. Public buckets violate the constraint, disabling output encryption exposes results, and inline datasets are not how evaluation jobs ingest data.

Source: Amazon Bedrock — Model evaluation — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/model-evaluation.html

Question: Two teams debate IAM design: Team A proposes one broad role with bedrock:* used by all services; Team B proposes per-service roles each limited to specific actions and model ARNs with conditions. For a security-sensitive, audited environment, which is correct and why?
Answer Option 1: Team B is wrong because IAM conditions cannot reference model ARNs
Answer Option 2: Team A, because bedrock:* is required for InvokeModel to function
Answer Option 3: Team A, because a single shared role simplifies CloudTrail since all activity appears under one identity
Answer Option 4: Team B, because per-service least-privilege roles limit blast radius, make CloudTrail attribution clear per service, and prevent unintended model or admin actions
Overall Explanation:
Distinct least-privilege roles scoped to required actions and model ARNs minimize blast radius and produce clear, per-service CloudTrail attribution, which is essential in an audited environment. A shared bedrock:* role grants far more than needed and muddies attribution, bedrock:* is not required for InvokeModel, and IAM resource ARNs/conditions do support scoping to specific models.

Source: Amazon Bedrock — Identity and access management — https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/userguide/security-iam.html
